coredata.dc:title: Usage of agarose gel waste for the high yield production of carbon dots and new insight into their toxicological screening
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, Pooja
Chaudhary, Savita
Bhasin, K.K.
coredata.dc:description: The production and application of carbon nanomaterials have engrossed much attention due to their fundamental characteristic properties. With the new advancement in optical and emission properties, the biotic platform has become an innovative boulevard to produce convenient and discriminatory strategies for CDs in biological application. Here, in this regard development of blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been carried out by applying thermal calcination process. The agar gel waste was utilized as a precursor because it is very difficult to dispose. The better control over the luminescence and physicochemical characteristics of CDs has inspired research work towards their commercialization. They possess excellent quantum yield value (68 %) which reflects their outstanding photoluminescence behaviour. The corresponding effect of formed CDs on haemolysis, blood clotting index, antioxidant and colloidal stability assay has been thoroughly investigated. The complete intactness of CDs in presence of different components of blood suggests the high compatibility. The higher antioxidant activities have supported the higher free radical scavenging activity of developed CDs. The developed CDs have displayed higher fluorescence selectivity towards dopamine (DA) with detection limit of 0.128 μM. and enhanced the potential aptitude in sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biocompatibility
Reduction
Antioxidant
Haemolysis
Blood clotting

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal calcination synthesis of waste agar gel to fabricate CD’s
In-vitro stability and colloidal activities of CD’s over essential blood components
Effect of CDs on haemolysis and blood clotting index
Hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging assay
In-vitro stability and colloidal activities of CDs over essential blood components
Effect of CDs on haemolysis and blood clotting index
Hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activity
Application of developed CD’s in dopamine sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence excitation spectrum and (c) TGA-DTA thermogram of developed CD’s.
(a) FESEM image and (b) HRTEM analysis of developed agarose waste derived CD’s.
(a) DSC thermogram, (b) XRD spectrum and (c) FTIR spectrum of developed CDs from waste agarose gel.

In vitro stability assay (a) in presence of different blood components (saline, BSA, cysteine and histidine), (b) variation in wavelength of CDs in presence of different pH buffer solutions (pH = 5.6, 6, 6.4 and 7.4) and Colloidal stability assay of CDs in presence of (c) glutamic acid, (d) HSA, (e) Adrenaline and (f) zein. The ascorbic acid has been used as control during the analysis.
Effect of different concentration of CDs on the (a) haemolysis ratio and (b) blood clotting index. (measurement performed at λ = 540 nm).
(a) Antioxidant activity, (b) hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activity and (c) total reducing capacity of CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of developed CDs in presence of different concentration of DA (1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700 and 800 μM) and (b) interference emission spectrum in presence of different biomolecules, amino acids and metal ions.
(a) stern-Volmer plot and B-H plot of developed CDs in the presence of different concentration of DA.
Recover study of developed CDs in the presence of different concentration of DA in different water mediums.
Pictorial representation for the development of waste agarose gel derived CD’s.

head.ce:title: Usage of agarose gel waste for the high yield production of carbon dots and new insight into their toxicological screening

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The production and application of carbon nanomaterials have engrossed much attention due to their fundamental characteristic properties. With the new advancement in optical and emission properties, the biotic platform has become an innovative boulevard to produce convenient and discriminatory strategies for CDs in biological application. Here, in this regard development of blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been carried out by applying thermal calcination process. The agar gel waste was utilized as a precursor because it is very difficult to dispose. The better control over the luminescence and physicochemical characteristics of CDs has inspired research work towards their commercialization. They possess excellent quantum yield value (68 %) which reflects their outstanding photoluminescence behaviour. The corresponding effect of formed CDs on haemolysis, blood clotting index, antioxidant and colloidal stability assay has been thoroughly investigated. The complete intactness of CDs in presence of different components of blood suggests the high compatibility. The higher antioxidant activities have supported the higher free radical scavenging activity of developed CDs. The developed CDs have displayed higher fluorescence selectivity towards dopamine (DA) with detection limit of 0.128 μM. and enhanced the potential aptitude in sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: From the last few years, nanotechnology has engrossed much attention due to their exploring potential application in the field of sensing of environmental contaminant [1]. Carbon dots (CDs), being one of the potential nanomaterial with unique fluorescence properties [2]. CDs exhibits a series of advantages including high water solubility, brilliant biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity as well as excellent electronic and optical properties as compared to semiconductor quantum dots [3]. They have wide range of application in the field of photocatalysis, drug delivery, bioimaging, cellular labelling and theragnostic designs [4]. Till date, CD’s can be synthesized successfully from different carbon containing precursors such as amino acids, polymers, glycols, kitchen waste, organic molecules, food materials, bacterial species and so on [5–9]. Depending upon the starting materials, the prepared CDs have displayed changes in optical and emission properties. The excitation dependent emission behaviour of CDs is also affected by the nature of coating materials [10]. In comparison to chemical synthesis, the usage of natural precursors for preparing CDs are found to be more beneficial due to their renewable and economical behaviour which convert waste biomass into valued and worthy material [11,12].

The better control over the biocompatibility and biodegradability rate of CDs have them effective material for in-vivo application especially in the tissue engineering and drug delivery therapy [13]. The modification in physical and chemical characteristic properties have enhanced the scope of CDs in nanomedicine [14]. However, various facts about the exact mechanism of biocompatible behavior of CDs are remaining unclear and various researches undergoing to investigate their characteristic performance which may endow their act [15]. In this regard, blood is being considered as the foremost fragment of in-vivo activities and composed of various components such as Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Human serum albumin (HSA), and saline medium along with different pH stabilities. Their colloidal binding ability has provided a new pathway for their detailed investigation in presence of CDs [16,17]. It is mandatory from biological point of view to investigate the substantial knowledge and influential behaviour of CDs in presence of blood components. For example, Ma et al. have synthesized N doped CDs from different leaves [18]. The formed particles have displayed higher antibacterial activities against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. However, detailed mechanistic aspect related to their biological activity was not explored in detail. In addition, the antioxidative capacity of CDs in biological systems were not fully explored [19]. In this view, the current work highlights the effect of CDs on haemolysis, blood clotting index and antioxidant properties for exploring biological performance. In addition, the fluorescence properties of CDs have been explored for sensing one of the important neurotransmitter i.e. Dopamine (DA). DA also known as 3,4-dihdroxyphenethylamine and holds impact on the functioning of cardiovascular, renal and central nervous system [20,21]. DA is synthesized from the decarboxylation process of 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine and mandatory for proper functioning of living system. The concentration level of endogenous DA is 0.01–1 μM in the human brain. However, the reduction in DA amount in living system leads to neurological diseases such as epilepsy, Parkinson’s, dementia, attention deficit and schizophrenia [22,23]. Till date, various methods such as capillary electrophoresis, high-performance liquid chromatography, chemiluminescence, electrochemical methods have been used for the determination of DA [22,23]. However, the high cost, time consuming processing, more sample requirement and expensive instrumentation have restricted their popularization [24]. In this regard, the application of fluorescence spectroscopy is considered as the most sensitive and selective analytical technique for the detection of DA.

In this work, the water soluble fluorescent CDs has been synthesized from the agar gel waste. The choice of agarose gel waste as precursor source was based to provide a better alternative for its disposal and conversion to a useful fluorescent probe for effective sensing. Agar waste is surplus non-volatile solid by-product which encompasses great quantity of polysaccharide constituents. Agarose gel is made up of d-galactose and 3,6-anhydro-l-galactopyranose units and considered as one of abundant and inexpensive biomass for preparing CDs. However, the extensive usage of agarose gel in antimicrobial, antifungal and electrophoresis studies in laboratorial and industrial scale has produced lot of issue of its disposal. The produced waste has been considered as most carcinogenic waste due to its adverse effect on living being [25–27]. Therefore, the current work provides an easy and effective mean to convert harmful toxin to useful CDs. The different biological activities such as haemolysis, blood clotting, antioxidant and colloidal stability assay have been used to explore the bio-congenial behaviour of developed CDs. The fluorescence properties of formed CDs have further been used for selective sensing of dopamine (DA) in aqueous media

Agar gel waste especially the electrophoresis and antimicrobial waste was obtained from Department of Environment Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh for the systhesis of CDs. A recombinant Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) and Human Serum Albumin (HSA) cell culture was procured from HIMEDIA with 99 % purity. The powdered form of Glutamic acid (C5H9NO4) and Zein protein were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich with ≥99 % purity (HPLC grade). l-Adrenaline (C9H13NO3) was acquired from HIMEDIA (≥99 % purity). Disodium hydrogen phosphate heptahydrate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate monohydrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. Sodium sulphate, ammonium molybdate; potassium ferricyanide and iron chloride were procured from Sigma Aldrich with 99.9 % purity. Ascorbic acid, dopamine and hydrogen peroxide were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Deionised water was used as a dispersing solvent for the preparation of samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal calcination synthesis of waste agar gel to fabricate CD’s
ce:section.ce:para:
The waste agarose gel was utilized as a predecessor constituent for the preparation of thermally-calcined CDs. Initially, 5 g of agar waste was placed in 50 mL silica crucible and heated in continuous manner at 300 °C for 3 h. After completion of reaction, the furnace was allowed to cool down at normal room temperature. The obtained black-brownish color ash was further allowed to grind and sieved by 0.20 mm mesh for making fine powder. Afterwards, the overnight stirring of 0.5 g of this powder in 50 mL deionised water was done at room temperature (Scheme 1 ). The homogenized solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 4 min and collected supernatant was dialysis using a dialysis bag of molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) 10 000 Da. Afterwards, the collected supernatant was allowed to heat at 130 °C for 1 h to obtain CDs. The respective sample was stored at 2 °C for further instrumental analysis and biological activities. The quantum yield (ɸ) values for the developed CDs was determined by considering Quinine sulfate and Rhodamine B dye as a reference standard material [28]. The aqueous suspension of respective dye was prepared and value of quantum yield was determined after applying the following formula: -
ɸ =
ɸ r
I x
I r
ɳ x 2
ɳ r 2
A r
A x
Where x subscript refers to the developed CDs, r refers to the standard dye, I correspond to the fluorescence emission intensity of the system, A refers to the optical absorbance values results from UV–vis absorption spectrum and ɳ refers to the refractive index of the material (ɳ = 1, as all the samples have been prepared in aqueous medium).

The absorption and binding behaviour of developed CDs in presence of different biological components (BSA, HSA and glutamic acid) was analysed using LABINDIA UV–vis. spectrophotometer with 1 cm quartz cell. The value of optical density was recorded at different wavelength values for each activity. The pH of the reaction media was checked by Mettler Toledo digital pH meter. The sonicator from Power Sonic 405 was utilized for the homogenous dispersion of components. The ultra-centrifugation of CDs was carried out by using REMI R-24 centrifuge in 15 mL vials. The laminar air flow with B.O.D incubator and HEPA (high efficiency particulates air) filters was utilized for the incubation of prepared samples. The hydrodynamic radii and polydispersity index of prepared CDs were examined on Malvern ZEN 1690 Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument. Perkin Elmer LS 55 spectrofluorometer was utilized was used to investigate the emission behavior of CDs. Hitachi FE-SEM instrument with energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was utilized to investigate the morphological of CDs. The topographical shape and size distribution profile of CDs was studied on high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) JEOL 2100 F instrument at 200 Kv. The nanocrystalline structural analysis and average size range of estimation of developed CDs was done on Panalytical X’Pert Pro powder X-ray diffractometer in scan range of 5−90° angle at a scan rate of 2 deg/min. The thermal behaviour of prepared CDs was investigated on thermogravimetric analytical instrument (TG-DTA) SDT Q-600 and Mettler Toledo (Q20) differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) instrument with scan range of 5 °C/min. Here, nitrogen gas was used as a purge gas (flow rate 50 mL/min). The presence of different functional groups on the surface of CDs was studied by using Perkin Elmer 400 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR).

The sensing aptitude of CDs were tested for the detection of DA in deionized water at pH = 7. Firstly, 1.5 mg (800 μM) of DA was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and allowed to sonicate for 15 min. Afterwards, the respective sample was kept at room temperature for 20 min for complete settlement. Further, serial dilution analysis of DA with different concentration ranges from 700, 600, 500, 400, 300, 200, 100, 50, 20, 10, 5 and 1 μM were prepared by utilizing deionized water. The fluorescence spectrum for each prepared sample was recorded in range of 200–800 nm at excitation wavelength of 310 nm and slit width of 10.0 nm [21,22]. Further, the selectivity and interference studies were evaluated in presence of various biomolecules including amino acids (Glycine, Glucose, Ascorbic acid, Urea, sucrose, l-Lysine, l-Phenylalanine, l-Serine, l-Valine, l-Leucine, l-cysteine, l-alanine) and metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Ba2+) with 800 μM concentration level. All the analysis was repeated trice for precision analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro stability and colloidal activities of CD’s over essential blood components
ce:section.ce:para: The in-vitro study has provided the insight potential of formed CDs in the field of biomedical application. Blood is considered most significant constituent of human and animal body. Therefore, the respective analysis of CDs was carried out in presence of essential blood components including BSA, histidine and cysteine. Here, water was taken as a reference standard solvent [29,30]. For the analysis, 2 % solution of BSA and 0.2 M solution of cysteine and histidine was prepared in 0.8 % saline solutions. The fresh phosphate buffers having pH 5.6, 6.5, 7 and 7.4 were prepared for diluting the solutions. Three different concentrations of CDs (500, 2500 and 5000 ppm) were used for studying the effect of prepared nanoparticles. For the measurements, equimolar ratio of CDs solutions was mixed with prepared solutions of blood components and allowed to incubate at 36 °C for 48 h. Afterwards, the absorption spectra of each sample was measurement with UV–vis. spectrophotometer. The colloidal permanency of developed CDs was tested with four different concentrations i.e. 10, 50, 100 and 500 ppm in presence of equal-volume ratio (1:1 mL) of glutamic acid, l-adrenaline, zein and HAS in phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4). The formed suspension was allowed to incubate at 37 °C for 24 h. The supernatant of respective solution was taken for calculating the binding or colloidal efficiency of formed CDs in presence of different biological components by using UV–vis. measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on haemolysis and blood clotting index
ce:section.ce:para:
The haemolysis effect of prepared CDs with different concentrations ranges i.e. (10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 mg) was tested on fresh blood sample collected in EDTA coated vials. The dilution of blood sample was done with 0.8 % saline solution. Afterwards, 0.2 mL of diluted blood solution was mixed with the known concentration of CDs solutions and allowed to incubate at 37 °C for 1 h on hot water plate [31,32]. Subsequently, the absorbance of collected supernatant via centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 4 min was noted at 540 nm for evaluating the released amount of haemoglobin from the sample. In addition, 0.2 M CaCl2 solution was slowly mixed with the freshly collected blood samples (0.5 mL) for calculating the blood clotting index. Later on, 0.2 mL solution of CD’s with different concentration was mixed to the above prepared blood suspension and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. Then after, 5 mL of deionized water was added to the mixture and allowed to centrifuge at 1000 rpm for 2 min. The optical density of dilute supernatant was measured at 542 nm. The following equation was used for calculating the haemolysis ratio and blood clotting index of CDs
Haemolysis ratio = (Asample – Anegative)/(Apositive-Anegative)
Blood clotting index = (Asample/Ablood) ×100
Where Asample corresponded to the optical density values of prepared CDs solution in contact of blood solution, Apositive refers to the absorbance value of blood samples diluted with water and Anegative corresponds to the absorbance of blood solution prepared in saline medium. Ablood is related to the absorbance value of diluted solution of fresh blood with deionized water.

In-vitro antioxidant performance of prepared CDs have given a direct perceptive towards the reduction efficiency of free radicals and enhanced their future scope in environmental remediation activities. The maximum antioxidant competence of ascorbic acid was used as a standard positive control during the analysis. Briefly describing, a stock solution was prepared by stirring an equimolar ratio of 0.6 M H2SO4 solution and 28 mM sodium sulphate solution with 4 mM ammonium molybdate solution at room temperature for 15 min [33–35]. Afterwards, the respective solution of CDs in methanol with different concentrations (500, 2500 and 5000 ppm) were mixed with stock solution and incubated at 90 °C for 90 min in dark condition. After incubation, the respective solutions were kept at room temperature for 30 min and color change was observed. A blank test sample was prepared in water without adding any CDs. The antioxidant amount was appraised by calculating the optical density at 695 nm [35]. All the measurements have been carried out in triplicate set and mean of average values were chosen for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging assay
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activity has also been utilized for estimating the scavenging behaviour of CDs as a function of different concentration i.e., 500, 2500 and 5000 ppm. In brief, 2 mL solution of 10 mM hydrogen peroxide in phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) was mixed with CDs suspension and allowed to stir at room temperature for 10 min [40,41]. The obtained solution was incubated at 40 °C for 1 h to complete reaction. A blank sample was also prepared in water medium. The optical density values were further utilized to calculate the percentage of hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity by employing the following equation.
(2)
S c a v e n g i n g
( % )
=
A c
-
A s
A c
× 100
Where Ac and As corresponded to the absorbance of control and test CDs samples respectively.

It is obligatory to investigate the reduction efficiency of formed CDs before utilizing them into environmental remediation activities. Here, aqueous suspension of CDs was prepared with different concentration i.e., 500, 2500 and 5000 ppm. For the analysis, 1 % phosphate buffer solution was prepared by mixing equimolar amount of sodium phosphate monobasic and sodium phosphate dibasic. Afterwards, 0.2 mM potassium ferricyanide aqueous solution was mixed with chosen concentrations of CDs in fixed proportion and allowed to incubate at 50 °C for 40 min under normal light [37]. After that, 10 % trichloroacetic acid aqueous solution was mixed with above incubated samples. The respective color variation was analyzed and suspension was further centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 10 min to obtain clear supernatant solution. Further, obtained supernatant was diluted with deionised water and mixed with 0.1 % ferric chloride solution. The optical density of the suspension was measured at 700 nm after stirring at room temperature for 10 min.

The current work presents the simple and one step thermal calcination procedure for fabricating economically viable fluorescent CDs from waste and toxic agarose gel. Different characterization techniques were utilized to investigate the properties of prepared CDs. The absorption spectral behavior of developed CDs has been explored with UV–vis. spectroscopy (Fig. 1 a). From the absorption spectrum, it was observed that two band were observed at 240 and 300 nm which corresponds to the π-π* and n-π* transition of different functional moieties presents on the exterior core surface of CDs. In addition, the absorbance values have displayed the linear increment with increasing the concentration of CDs (Fig S1a). This was mainly explained due to the presence of more number of absorbing unit with increasing the concentrations of CDs from 2 to 4 mg. The fluorescence measurements were done to explore the emission behaviour of developed CDs in working range of 200−800 nm (Fig. 1b). It was observed that on increasing the excitation wavelength from 250 to 310 nm, the emission intensity increased linearly with slight red shift [28,39]. Afterwards, there was observable decrement in the emission intensity with excitation at 320 nm. Therefore, the maximum emission intensity was observed at 550 nm by exciting the sample at 310 nm wavelength. The value of quantum yield was found to be 62 and 56 % at 310 nm excitation wavelength with Rhodamine B and Quinine sulfate dye as a standard reference respectively [28,39]. Additionally, the concentration dependent effect was also studied to explore the emission behaviour of CDs (Fig. S1b). The intensity was linearly increased with increasing the amount of CDs in reaction media. The thermal decomposition and stability of the prepared CDs have been tested using thermo gravimetric analysis under temperature range of 0−1000 °C (Fig. 1c). Three major weight losses of 28, 12 and 59 % were observed at 75, 349 and 414 °C. The first weight loss aroused due to the evaporation of water molecules and moisture content present in CDs. The second weight loss occurred due to the evaporation of different functional groups present on the exterior core surface of CDs. The third weight loss took place due to the evaporation of different pyro gas molecules (carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide and methanol) present on the shallow of CDs along with the breakage of loosely bound molecules [38,39]. From here, it can be concluded that total 99 % decomposition of CDs occurred in the system.

In addition, the size analysis and homogenous behavior of particles was characterized by DLS instrument (Fig. S2). The value of hydrodynamic radii and polydispersity index (PDI) for the developed CDs was found to be 12 nm and 0.3 respectively. The obtained PDI value suggested the highly dispersed, uniform and homogenous nature of developed particles. Further, zeta potential analysis for the developed CDs has been carried out (Fig. S3). The water was used as a dispersant medium whose value of refractive index and viscosity was 1.33 and 0.8872. The value of zeta potential and conductivity of CDs was found to be −14 mV and 0.0128 mS/cm. The morphological alteration of developed CDs was studied by using FE-SEM analysis (Fig. 2 a). The CD sample has possessed small spherical size particles with resemblance to each other in assorted ways. The topological alteration and size distribution profile of the fabricated CDs was measured by low resolution (Fig. S4) and high resolution (HRTEM) analysis (Fig. 2b). The obtained TEM micrograph displayed monodispersed and uniform size distribution. The size of CDs has been found in nanometric range 8−10 nm.

Further, the heating behaviour of developed CDs was investigated by using DSC analysis (Fig. 3 a). Three exothermic peaks at 37.28, 118.03 and 153.07 °C have displayed different heat of enthalpy values viz 111.8, 37.64 and 10.93 J/g. From here, it can be inferred that developed CDs have exothermic and non-spontaneous behavior. Powdered X-ray diffraction study was utilized to explore the nanocrystalline nature of developed CDs (Fig. 3b). The observed spectrum reflects diffraction peaks at 2θ = 17.39, 20.80, 24.44, 27.80 and 38.48° which corresponds to the (100), (110), (002), (111) and (101) diffracting planes. The size of prepared particles was found to be 20.67 nm by using Debye Scherrer equation (D = Kλ/βCosɵ). The value of dislocation density was 0.0023 nm−1 which suggested the presence of different defects on the surface developed CDs. The obtained peaks suggested the highly exfoliated graphitic nature of CDs and demonstrating the carbon core construction of material. The strong peak at 24.44° was mainly corresponded to the conjugate sp2 hybridized carbon in the associated structure and present in the aromatic ring structure. From here, it was clear that CDs have nano-crystalline graphic structure. The presence of different functional groups on the exterior core of CDs has been confirmed by FTIR spectroscopic method (Fig. 3c). The characteristic peak at 3478 and 2916 cm−1 corresponded to the strong hydroxyl (OH), amine (NH) groups and CN stretching vibration in sample [28,38,39]. The CH stretching and CO stretching vibration were observed at 2848 and 1465 cm−1. The band at 1220 and 805 cm−1 referred to the strong CC and CH stretching vibration. The respective peak at 967 cm−1 refers to the epoxy and ether functional groups. From here, it can be concluded that developed CDs have possible carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine functional groups present on the surface of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro stability and colloidal activities of CDs over essential blood components
ce:section.ce:para: The in-vitro and colloidal stability in presence of different biological components have been explored to widen the practical scope of prepared CDs in in-vivo application. The analysis by measuring the adsorbing capacity of essential blood components (BSA, cysteine, histidine, saline, HSA, glutamic acid, zein protein and l-adrenaline) in presence of different concentration (500, 2500 and 5000 ppm) of CDs (Fig. 4 a) [23,36]. The corresponding influence of different pH on the various blood components have also been tested in presence of CDs (Fig. 4b). The presence of chosen concentrations of CDs have not displayed any kind of visual or aggregation changes in model blood components. The outcomes have supported the in-vitro stability of blood components in presence of CDs even at very high concentration level. To quantify the results, UV–vis. spectroscopic measurements were carried out for the respective samples. From the obtained graph, it was observed that in presence of different blood components, the wavelength of CDs solution has shown shift within 10 nm range. The results has further verified the in-vitro stability of formed particles towards blood components (Fig. 4a). The data has further been compared in presence of different buffer solutions with pH varies from 5.6–7.4 (Fig. 4b). The variations were found to be comparable with the earlier results. The slight bathochromic and hypsochromic shift was observed in presence CDs which were found to be in permissible range and further suggested the biocompatible behavior of formed CDs. However, for the colloidal stability, the characteristic absorbance peak of CDs varies slightly in presence of HSA, zein, l-adrenaline (ADR) and glutamic acid (Fig. 4c–f). In presence of glutamic acid, the prepared CDs have displayed two characteristic bands at 234 and 284 nm respectively. However, in presence of HSA, the CDs have shown a significant band at 284 nm. The UV–vis band for CDs was positioned at 240 nm in presence of zein. The absorbance values have displayed the slight enhancement with increasing the concentration from 10 to 100 ppm in all chosen biological components. However maximum absorbance was observed in presence of 500 ppm of CDs. This was mainly explained due to the maximum association of biological component over the exterior surface of CDs. Therefore, 500 ppm amount of CDs was found to be appropriate for attaining maximum adsorption value from the chosen biological components. In case of adrenaline (ADR), slightly different behaviour was observed in presence of CDs. The characteristic peak of CDs has displayed the maximum shift of 85 nm in presence of adrenaline. This behavioural variation has suggested that disturbance in the optical properties of CDs which further leads to decrement in electronic transitions. From all these results it can be concluded that the formed particles have not displayed any negative impact over the essential components of blood. The particles structure was also not varied much in presence of blood components and displayed the excellent colloidal stability except adrenaline. The results have supported the biocompatibility of formed CDs and their potential scope biosensing of molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on haemolysis and blood clotting index
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of developed CDs on blood was further visualized by utilizing in-vitro haemolysis assay as a function of concentration variation of CDs from 10 to 30 ppm. The concentration variation has produced the significant effect over the haemolysis ratio. For instance, the presence of 10 ppm amount of CDs has produced minimum value of haemolysis ratio i.e., 2 %. This ratio was linearly increased with increasing the amount of CDs in the system and attained maximum permissible value of 7 % in presence of 30 ppm of CDs (Fig. 5 a). Simultaneously, the blood clotting index was estimated for verifying the significance of chosen CDs in biomedical application. On interpreting the results, it was observed that the value of blood clotting index was quite high and vary marginally with increasing the amount of CDs in the reaction system (Fig. 5b). The outcomes of the work have clearly verified that the prepared CDs have not distressed the blood clothing mechanism. Therefore, the developed CDs have been considered as the biocompatible material as a function of dosage amount and have the possessed potential to be used for various biomedical applications in future.

Antioxidant activity engrossed much attention in the field of biological and environmental science. It basically deals with the reduction capacity of free radical species present in the environment. Free radical species causes oxidation stress which alters the activities of lipids, DNA and proteins, results in the introduction of various diseases. Antioxidant agents provide a shield against potentially active harmful free radicals in dose dependent manner. Therefore, it is indispensable to evaluate the antioxidant competence of formed CDs. Here, very high concentration of CDs (500, 2500 and 5000 ppm) were used to enhance the capability of formed particles (Fig. 6 a). From the visual inspection, it was observed that the transparent color of CDs were changed to light greenish blue color during antioxidant activity analysis. In additon, the antioxidant capacity of prepared CDs was slightly decreased on increasing the concentration of CDs from 500 to 5000 ppm. The reason of slight decrement might be due to the increased adsorption efficency of higher concentration of CDs. However, the agar waste derived C’s have the potential to imitate the prospective of free radical apprehension in dose dependent manner. The presence of different fuctional groups on the surface of CDs has further attributed to the maximum antioxidant activity of formed particles. Thus, it can be concluded that, developed CDs have exhibited greater antioxidant capacity at 500 ppm level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para: The free radical scavenging acitivity of CDs in presence of hydrogen peroxide has reflected their potential usage in biomedical applications. To better understand the mechanistic behaviour of formed CDs, the respective UV–vis. spectroscopy was taken into consideration in the wavelength range of 200−800 nm and the value of optical density at 290 nm wavelength was used to to calculate the percentage of scavenging activity (Fig. 6b). From the obtained results, it was inferred that on increasing the concentration of CDs from 500 to 5000 ppm in hydrogen peroxide solution, the percentage of free radical scavenging activity has shown noticable decrease. The outcomes are in good agreement with in-vitro antioxidant assay. The higher concentration of CDs has reduced the activity of hydrogen peroxide due to the rupturing of peroxide bond linkage in the system. Therefore, it can be concluded that 500 ppm CDs has exhibited strong hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activity.

The reduction efficiency deals with the free radical species produced during various biological activities. The production of free radical species has the tendency to affect the life cycle of various living species. Therefore its important to evaluate the reducing ability of prepared CDs before environmental exposure. For the analysis, the optical density was measured at 420 nm and 700 nm as a function of different concentation of CDs (Fig. 6c). On comparison with the control system (L-Ascorbic acid), the value of optical density was found to be slightly increased with increasing the concentration of the CDs and suggested the increased reduction power of formed CDs. The maxium reduction efficiency of 5000 ppm CDs can be further be stimulated in the field of free radical species and made the formed particles as an outstanding reducing and biologically active reagent.

From the above mentioned activities, it can be inferred that developed CDs have possesed highly biocompatible nature. Firstly the in-vitro and colloidal stability of developed C’s has been investigated and 500 ppm concentration has been found most colloidally stable concentration. The haemolysis assay and blood clotting index activity has been evaluated for exploring the intact compatibility of prepared CDs. During haemolysis assay, 30 ppm concentration was found most appropriate as it has maximum lysis ability towards blood and correspondingly it has minimum blood clotting index capacity. The maximum clotting index was found in case of 10 ppm. From the antioxidant activity, 500 ppm concentration of CDs have reflected maximum radical scavenging activity. Therefore, 500 ppm concentration has been considered as most appropriate and have maximum adsorption tendency for biological components. From here it can be concluded that higher concentration of CDs have maximum bio-congenial and non-toxic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of developed CD’s in dopamine sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is considered as the most significant and imperative neurotransmitter. It improves the pumping ability of heart which provides excllent blood flow to kidney. It is used to cure various diseases such as epilepsy, Parkinson’s, dementia, and schizophrenia in human beings. Therefore, it is mandatory to maintain the level of DA in our body. Hence, the estimation of DA via using cost-effective and efficient sensor is highly recommended. Here, in this paper fluorescence spectroscopy has been utilized to determine the level of DA because of its various advantages such as low cost, more selectivity and sensitivity, high performance and less sample requirement.

The analytical performance of developed fluorescent probe was evaluated against DA. It was observed that significant amount of quenching in emission intensity of CDs was observed in case of DA (800 μM). However, the emission intensity increased linearly via decreasing the concentration of DA from 800 to 5 μM (Fig. 7 a). However, the emission intensity of 1 μM concentration of DA was perfectly matched with the intensity of CDs. From here, it can be inferred that, the developed fluorescent CDs can detect DA up to 1 μM concentration level. The developed sensor acts as eco-friendly and simple in nature. It was observed that the amine group of DA gets protonated and changed into ammonium ion in neutral medium. Additionally, CDs have possessed the negative surface charge due to the presence of negatively charged oxygen containing group (carboxyl and hydroxyl). Further, non-covalent interaction involving electrostatic and H-bonding between DA and CDs has produced quenching effect. The lone pair present on the oxygen containing groups have produced intramolecular charge transfer and photoinduced electron transfer in the system which further reduced the emission intensity. Additionally, the negligible overlap between the absorption spectra of DA with the emission spectrum of CDs ahs recued the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET). From here it can be concluded that quenching phenomena of DA results from photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Additionally, the agglomeration of CDs due to the non-covalent interaction between hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine groups of CDs and hydroxyl and amine group of DA has produced quenching in emission intensity. In alkaline medium, DA changes to DA quinone which have very high electron trapping tendency and it can easily accept electrons generated from CDs. Therefore, it can be inferred that DA get oxidized to DA quinone in alkaline medium and results in fluorescence quenching.

The selectivity and interference performance of the respective system has been carried out after authenticating the quenching phenomena of DA (Fig. 7b). Various potential interferents such as biomolecules (Glycine, Glucose, Ascorbic acid, Urea, sucrose), amino acids (L-Lysine, l-Phenylalanine, l-Serine, l-Valine, l-Leucine, l-cysteine, l-alanine) and metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Ba2+) has been taken into consideration at 800 μM concentration level. From the emission spectrum, it was observed that the developed CDs shown very slight and negligible quenching in the presence of respective interferants and maximum quenching was only observed in case of DA which reflects the ultimate selectivity and specificity of CDs towards DA. The synchronicity of other interferants with DA does not affect the quenching ability of the developed sensing probe. To verify, the relative emission intensity plot (Io/I vs system) was drawn. The corresponding fluorescence spectrum of the respective system has been represented in Fig S5a. Simultaneously, normalized intensity plot ((Io-I)/I vs system) were also drawn to determine the selectivity of DA (Fig. S5b). The stern-Volmer graph between Fo/F vs. concentration (where Fo and F refers to the intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of DA) and B-H plot between 1/Fo-F vs 1/concentration have given the value of 0.128 μM as detection limit and 0.0024 as binding constant (Fig. 8 ).

The recovery studies of DA were also tested in real water sample taken from different sources such as lake water, well water, tap water, rain water and sewerage water for analyzing the practical application of developed CDs (Fig. 9 ). The standard addition method was utilized in the presence of various real water samples. Different concentration of DA such as 5, 10, 20, 50 and 100 μM used in presence of developed CDs. The recovery rate was found between 85–95 % which demonstrates the potential ability of CDs towards DA in different mediums. The obtained results have reflected the potential aptitude of sensor in DA sensing. Therefore, it can be inferred that the developed sensor shown promising application with low response time.

In summary, a simple, selective, sensitive thermal calcination method has been developed for the preparation of CDs from waste agarose gel. Different characterization techniques have been utilized to explore the optical and emission characteristics of developed CDs with size range of 8−10 nm. The biocompatible nature and influential behaviour of developed CDs on different blood components has been explored through haemolysis and blood clotting index activity. The anti-oxidative amount of CDs has been evaluated by measuring the total antioxidant capacity. Further, colloidal stability of CDs has been investigated in presence of different biological components and found fully intacted in nature. The developed CDs have been found highly bio-congenial in nature. Further, developed CDs shown ultimate selectivity towards DA in presence of various biomolecules, amino acids and metal ions with detection limit value of 0.128 μM. The obtained results clearly established the promising protagonist in modifying the toxicological performance of CDs. The tentative studies in the current paper evaluate the competence of possible threats and fulfil the different uncertainties about the benign use of CDs in biological field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Authors are thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL and Department of Environment Studies Panjab University Chandigarh for technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.procbio.2021.01.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
